title: Cephaelis ombrophila
taxonomy: Cephaelis ombrophila is a species of flowering plant in the family Rubiaceae, commonly known as the coffee family. This plant belongs to the genus Cephaelis, which comprises approximately 200 species. Cephaelis ombrophila is native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, where it grows as an understory plant in the rainforest. The exact classification of Cephaelis ombrophila is still being studied and refined by botanists. Further research is needed to fully understand the taxonomic relationships within this species.
uses: Cephaelis ombrophila is known for its medicinal properties, particularly in the treatment of various health conditions. Traditionally, the plant has been used to alleviate symptoms associated with fever, colds, and flu. Its extracts have been researched for their potential antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, which could help combat infections and reduce inflammation in the body. Furthermore, Cephaelis ombrophila is believed to possess antioxidant properties, which may help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. More research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential applications.

parts: Cephaelis ombrophila is a plant species that is used in traditional medicine, particularly in Central and South America. intro: Cephaelis ombrophila, also known as the West Indian or Brazilian bitter, is a plant species that belongs to the Rubiaceae family. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits, which include the treatment of digestive issues, such as dyspepsia and diarrhea, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Cephaelis ombrophila include alkaloids, such as ambrox, and other compounds that contribute to its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a bitter tonic, typically in the form of a liquid extract or a tea, to stimulate digestion and support overall gastrointestinal health.
